title: Jordan Taylor's Mama Issues
preview: “I was going to be a successful Entrepreneur dating all the women I wanted except for Black women. Men have mama issues and lessons we missed too. It wasn’t that I was against them I was just a product of my circumstances. Growing up in...
body:
“I was going to be a successful Entrepreneur dating all the women I wanted except for Black women. Men have mama issues and lessons we missed too. It wasn’t that I was against them I was just a product of my circumstances. Growing up in Iowa left me few choices. I had to date the women that were available. So, for me the grass always looked greener on the other side of the fence. My dating choices soon revealed to me that not once but twice I had crossed the wrong fence”.

Jordan Taylor 32 years old, Cedar Rapids, Iowa

II

was born and raised in Iowa with my mother, two sisters, one brother and a dad who was absent in and out of our lives due to work. It’s important to note that my mother was Black and my father who sometimes (MIA), missing in action was White. Some things in life you just have no control over. Choosing your parents is one of them. Technically, there is no such thing as a Black or White person. That’s just how we describe and label people in the Un-United States of America. In this Country race is a social construct. Historically, color became race and race became power. And that’s where and why the divide exists.
My father and mother both worked for the railroad that’s how they met. When the kids started coming, she was a stay-at-home mom. My dad was gone most of the time on the road and God only knows what life he was living while he was away. One thing tho, he did provide financially. Sometimes, your presence is important too. Mom was our stable caretaker. She made sure that we had the basics and that we lived a fairly comfortable life. There’s only one thing. My mom (like a lot of others) taught my two sisters by preparing them for life.
She made sure they learned how to clean, cook, wash the dishes, do the laundry, iron, and anything else that had to do with taking care of the household including their two brothers. She raised her daughters and spoiled her sons (hard on the girls easy on the boys). My dad grew up in the same type of home. The girls did the work, and the boys were catered too. It was the usual stereotype. The men mowed the lawn and took the garbage out. He had no problem with how things were run. This was social conditioning at its best. When he was at home, he wanted to be spoiled too.
I believe that’s why my dad worked so hard. Not only did he want us to enjoy a good life, I think he was content that he had a strong woman that was a supportive help mate. She ran the house like a tight ship, and everything was always in order. He was well aware that she favored the boys more. I could hear him telling her not to treat us differently. He tried to explain to her that we had to learn how to do things for ourselves. We would have to go out into the unsheltered real world one day. We wouldn’t have a chance to survive. It was a possibility that his sons may not find that woman that would take care of them. That just fell on deaf ears.
I think sometimes he let a lot of things slide because he didn’t really have a clue on how to raise a Black boy or if he even knew what reality was like for us. He was actually learning right along with us. He knew he could not teach us how to be a Black man in America. There was no way he could know our struggles if he never experienced it his self. We needed to be taught that we have to compete and, in most cases, have to be better academically and athletically. We needed to be aware of how to react to racially motivated violence coming from the police or what to say and do when detained. We may never have the same privileges and rights that our father has been entitled or privileged to. Or, even have the same opportunities.
There are even certain clothes we can’t wear because we could be mistaken for someone committing a crime or even be shot. America judges me, other young Black men and boys on the surface. As a man we are measured by our personal appearance. No chains, hoodies, wife beaters (white sleeveless t-shirts), sagging pants, tattoos, natural hair styles are suspect. And the list goes on. Wearing or displaying any of these could mean death or a lengthy sentence in jail. Most Black folk have experienced this. We automatically, without asking to have a target on our backs. Some people will marginalize and reject us for the color of our skin. I believe all of these thoughts and how to explain so many lessons frightened and angered him! If only his anger could possibly even the playing ground. It hasn’t in over 400 years!
What exactly did mom do that was so bad? She tried to run our lives by controlling just about every move (what we wore, where we went, who we were with, our coming and going). She tried to turn us into pussy whipped mama’s boys coddling us so we couldn’t do anything or think for ourselves. We Had to depend on her. In the process of instilling values, morals, and responsibilities into her daughters, she forgot that her sons needed to learn those same things.
Discipline was the other thing. It was inconsistent. It was a hit or miss. When my sisters would talk back or have an attitude, all hell broke loose. But when her precious sons misbehaved, there were no consequences. Punishments were rare just warnings. When dad was there, she tried to shield us by pleading with him. That wasn’t happening and we were dealt with. He did understand that structure was just as important for boys as it was for girls.
Mom did a grave disservice by doing this. What my brother and I learned was to expect women to cater to us. It seems like it’s the same old adage men shouldn’t have to do women’s work. Why because some mothers are preparing their daughters to be wives. Of course this is not true in all cases. Sometimes, we are just conditioned by how our parents were brought up. In some home environments various skills are explicitly gender specific and girls are supposed to do more domestic chores than boys. In our culture masculine roles are generally associated with strength, aggression, and dominance. Feminine roles are generally associated with passivity, nurturing and subordination. Gender role socialization begins and birth and continues throughout a lifetime. All that babying stuff worked for my brother but not for me. That’s why a lot of young men are still living at home in mama’s house.
They don’t feel that have to work or be responsible because mama has always come to their rescue. They feel a sense of entitlement and that a woman should take care of them. And guess what? There are plenty of women out here that will! I was the defiant son and would always rebel. Something in me always fought for independence. Thus, began the love hate relationship between me and my mom. As I grew older, I became one sighted and only saw Black women as overprotective, overbearing domineering mother figures. So, I steered clear of them like the plague. For me, the grass always looked greener on the other side. And those were the women I desired. Our dad did step up. He continued to learn, read, and ask questions in order to raise us and we kind of figured it out why he was gone so much because of his fear that he would fail his children and guide his kids in the wrong direction. After all, what could a white man teach us? Dad did do the best he could. With his love and support we all managed to escape most of the cruelties growing up Black. My sisters and I attended college in Iowa where we graduated with bachelor’s degrees. That spoiled brat brother of mine stayed home with his mommy and worked various jobs.  I graduated with a degree in Global Business Management/Finance. I was always interested in the Import Export Business. It took me a little longer, but I made it. While I was in school, I became great friends with a classmate who went through the same Business program. It was definitely to my benefit because he had all the right connections for us to set up a successful business.
Rick and I decided that we were going into partnership sometime after graduation. We agreed that we were going to export soybeans to Southeast Asia in Malaysia. Our Export company Midland Soybeans International was going to be the leading company in the U.S. We were off and running because we had all the connections and mentors that were needed for us to make big bank! When we were ready, they would help us to get our business going. A lightbulb should have gone off in my head because I didn’t know anything about Rick’s connections or associates. I was just following blindly into this future partnership. I was the only Black person in this organization which made me feel uneasy.
Rick assured me that everything would be fine. You know the saying when something feels too good to be true it usually is! Advice to self always listen to your inner voice.
Rick and I decided that more business classes were needed to expand our knowledge in the International Trade business. So, we decided to enroll at a University in Minnesota. This was going to be a fun journey for us. We spent most of our lives in Iowa, so it was time for a new adventure. The classes we enrolled in were interesting but there was something more interesting that caught my eyes. Her name was Fawn. She had the most beautiful indigenous woman I had ever seen, and she was my professor. I knew from that first day I wanted to be with her. I knew it was unethical to date your professor. I was going to find a way to make it happen. Rick and I studied hard, and we partied just as hard. One night I decided to visit a club that was in a suburb about 20 miles from campus. It was supposed to be more of an older crowd. Rick wasn’t interested so I went ahead. I was always attracted to women a few years older than me and thought I might meet one. Women my age were always so clingy and immature.
The music was blasting as I pulled up. Inside, people were having a good time at the bar, and some were dancing. I scanned the room checking out the ladies. What luck, sitting at the bar was Professor Denton with one of her friends. I stood back and watched as she sipped her drink. I approached her and said good evening. Her speech was a little slurred, but she did acknowledge me, and she actually knew my name. There was a seat next to hers and she motioned me to sit. She introduced me to her friend Nita, and we engaged in a little conversation. The DJ was playing one of my favorites and I asked Fawn to dance. She had some great moves, and I watched her body closely, every movement. She was so sensual and sexy. As we talked a little more, she drank a little more. She was having a good time, so I tried not to think too much of it. It was getting late, and I said my good-byes. Before I left Fawn let me know that she came here on weekends and that maybe we could hang out sometime. Was it possible that my professor was flirting with me? In my wildest dreams probably not.
The next day in class was a little awkward. The fact that I had a good time the night before and the fact that I saw Fawn in a different light. The class lecture about Trade protocol was met on deaf ears. Spending whatever time with her was all I could think about. The weekend couldn’t get here fast enough. When I told Rick about the club, he shook his head and said I was asking for trouble. There is no way that a beautiful intelligent woman could be trouble or, could she?
We spent a few more meetings hangin’ out at the club. Each time I noticed her drinking habits, but I didn’t care I was just happy to be spending time with her. This night she had more to drink than usual. She could hardly talk let alone walk. Certainly, I couldn’t let her drive in the condition she was in. There was only one gentlemanly thing to do and that was to see she got home safely.
Fawn’s apartment was cozy with nice furnishings and decorated with beautiful Native artifacts. I brought her in and carefully put her body on the sectional sofa. She was definitely out of it and had consumed more than she should have. I looked at her lying there unconscious to the world. As the door closed behind me, I wondered what inner pain drove her to drink this way.
When I got home, I told Rick what happened. He started throwing racial insults right away. He said, “You know those drunken Indians can’t hold their liquor and, “Don’t you realize she could’ve scalped you.? Then, he put his hands over his mouth and was mocking the call they make (OooooOooooo) like you used to hear in the cowboy Indian movies. I found out later this was really a war cry or chant. Oh, he was laughing and having a good ole time. I stopped his fun immediately and punched him in the face. I had never seen this type of talk before from him about another race. He was always cool. Something that night ticked me off. Maybe because he was white and most times, they feel they can just say whatever they want with no repercussions. He couldn’t understand why I didn’t want to date a woman of my own race. I posed the same question to him. Because Rick only dated Black women. It had explained to him many times before that my upbringing with my mom caused me to move in another direction when it came to women.
He told me he only dated Black women because they were more mature, independent, not so silly, and clingy. He did apologize and realized he was being a jerk. He never did that again with any other woman I chose to date. Just a side note. It’s important that we all understand that alcoholism is not specific to anyone particular group of people. It crosses all races whether it be an individual or family problem! We need to get away from stereotypes and generalizations about others. Well, who knows when or if that will ever happen.
Monday morning couldn’t get here fast enough, nor could I get to class fast enough. I wanted to know that Fawn was alright and that I would find her standing in front of the podium sober. Concentration was difficult and Rick kept nudging me to stay focused. Class was just about over, and I heard her say “Mr. Taylor, please see me in my office.” When I got there the door was opened and she ushered me in.
Mr. Taylor, I find myself in an embarrassing situation. You do know what I’m talking about? I nodded and waited to see what she would say. Fawn explained that sometimes her drinking gets the best of her. It just helps me relax after the pressures of being the only Native American Professor in the Department. The other professors give me a difficult time and there is more stress from them to get tenured. Without it I can’t become a full-time Professor. Basically, she just wanted to thank me for getting her home safely.
Then, the shocker came! Fawn invited me over for dinner. My response was quick and decisive. It was decided that it would be that weekend, and she promised to prepare a traditional Native meal. I already knew what Rick was going to say, “You are asking for trouble.” Did I care or listen? No.
The 25 miles of driving to Fawn’s place seemed forever. I didn’t know how the evening would play out, but I was open and receptive to whatever happened. When she opened the door all I could see was beauty and class. She was dressed in her Native Regalia which is traditional clothing used for ceremonial occasions. Some might refer to it as a costume. This is totally rude because of its long association with greedy corporations. They profit from these tactless red-faced costumes sold and worn on Halloween.
Capitalism will make those in power steal others cultural clothing and even steal their land too. I could tell Fawn had already been drinking but, again I looked past that and was just glad to be with her. The dinner that was prepared was interesting. I knew about the wild rice with sweet potatoes and fried bread. The other dishes weren’t as familiar, and I prayed that I would be able to eat them. What was familiar was the drinking that I had become accustomed too. Guess she felt comfortable around me and was able to share some personal things in her life. I asked her what her name meant. She told me a deer meant a warrior-like spirit. But deer are also gentle and graceful too. I could see that in the way she moved with strength confidence and gracefulness.
Fawn’s story is a historic one. In case you didn’t know, you’re ‘bout to get schooled. Between 1869 and the 1960’s, hundreds of thousands of Native American children were removed violently from their homes and families and placed in boarding or mission schools operated by the federal government and churches. They were housed there sometimes four or more years. The children were forced to wear basic uniforms. Overalls, shirts, and pants were common for the boys and the girls wore white dresses. They had to cut their hair, speak their language, and learn the ways of those with less melanin. Hmmmmm, who were the savages?
It seems that Fawn’s grandparents and parents were subjected to these cruel conditions. Not to mention living on restricted land so graciously given to them by the government called reservations. Drinking became their means of survival. They suffered from poverty, malnutrition, and very low standards of living. The Europeans came here uninvited bringing disease and destruction. I understood now why she drank. It was to numb the pain. No amount of alcohol could ever erase the horrific treatment that the Natives experienced! I was so glad to be there to wipe her tears away. All I wanted to do was comfort her. Comforting led to hugging; hugging led to kissing and kissing well that led to the bedroom. Passion can make you do crazy things and make bad choices like having unprotected sex. One time is a mishap but, more than one is just plain stupid. Sleeping with my professor was another bad decision. I fucked up literally. Now, I wondered what was going to happen next. It looks like Rick might have been right.
The light in the window was bright. It let me know that it was morning. Fawn woke up and was not too happy about what happened last night. I guess it hit her too that she had slept with her student and had unprotected sex. Even though we both realized the road we were traveling was a dangerous one, it didn’t stop us from seeing each other. It’s no fun being in a relationship when you have to sneak around. You are always watching to see that no one is watching you. This went on for months and so did the drinking. Fawn and I would come to our senses every now and then and say this had to end because we were playing with fire when it came to her job. I was still enrolled in her class and that pressure caused her to drink even more. We were fighting and I mean physical fighting and arguing more than ever.
One, night, Fawn started thinking back to her roots and where she grew up. She wanted to talk about it so of course I listened. Her parents worked hard as laborers. Both of them worked at a hotel cleaning rooms and as a laundry room assistant. Quite frequently they were met with racial slurs by some of the guest. This humiliation and disrespect stripped her father of his dignity.
It was difficult for him knowing that he was barely providing for his family, and his manhood was constantly being challenged. As a Native man this led him on frequent drinking sprees until finally, he lost his job. Her mom continued working at the hotel and this caused many arguments and fights. Her three brothers worked at odd jobs as adults. She wanted a better life and was the only one in her family that went on to college to receive a degree. She remembered some of her mother’s friends and aunts that used to meet weekly for drumming. Fawn thought that maybe finding a drumming group would help reconnect to her inner self, the heartbeat of the people and Mother Earth. She explained to me that drums gave Native Americans a way to connect to the gods to contact their inner spirits and be healed. How ironic that both of our cultures celebrate the power of drumming. African drumming was used to communicate, celebrate, mourn, and inspire. They were played in times of peace, war, planting, harvesting, birth, and death. Drums have always been a large part of Africans’ experiences as well. They both are considered sacred and represent the pulse and heartbeat of the people. She asked me not to give up on her and maybe this was the answer to her problems. Only God could help her through this. She had to realize that God will help those who help themselves.
The first night Fawn came home from drumming circle it was as if her entire spirit had been lifted. I started thinking maybe this could work. I wanted us to stay together and possibly build a future. This was probably a childish thought. Without her family’s support I knew the journey would be a hard one. She had not communicated with her mom and dad in several years. She couldn’t handle the drinking in the house and the fighting all the time. She blamed her mother for staying and putting up with it. Each week Fawn seemed to drink less and less, and I was excited that things were getting better. She even talked me into attending a powwow. She said this would also help even more to bring her back to her roots. I heard about them but had never experienced one. A powwow is a celebration of American Indian culture in which people from diverse indigenous nations gather for the purpose of dancing, singing, and honoring the traditions of their ancestors. I had the best time. All the different tribal people coming together and sharing their culture was uplifting. The music, dancing and was something to see. The smell of buffalo or bison burgers was not the highlight of my attendance.
Well, it seemed like Fawn was taking the steps toward recovery until she found out her father had died. The guilt consumed her because she had stopped communicating with her family. She just wasn’t strong enough to handle it and started drinking again. The arguments started again, and we finally decided that this relationship was not healthy physically, mentally or emotionally for her or for me. Let’s just call it what it is. It’s not that easy and one or two triggers can send you back where you were.
Fawn was a high functioning alcoholic but still an alcoholic. I understood that her father was one. And, growing up, this is what she experienced in her home. Fighting and arguing unfortunately was very common there. Those memories were a constant reminder and now she was living that same life.
At this point, there was nothing to do but stop this craziness. The fights were draining and fortunately, none of this ever got back to her job. And she wasn’t kicked out of the apartment. on several occasions the neighbors made sure they called the cops. I was never in an abusive relationship before, and I didn’t need to start now. It takes a lot of energy to keep a fired-up woman from throwing punches and trying to slap and scratch you. It is so true when you see only the outside of a person. Eventually, you will see the inside and who that person really is. That isn’t always a pretty picture. Research has shown that when someone suffers from alcoholism, they experience significant changes in the brain. These changes affect a person’s actions and make them extremely difficult to control. I watched her changes affect a person’s actions and makes them extremely difficult to control. I watched her suffer each time she took a drink, and her suffering hurt me too.
People on campus were beginning to talk. Rick let me know that some of the students had seen us out together. And of course, the tongues were wagging. Once the Administration became aware of all this talk Fawn would surely lose her job. I didn’t want that to happen. As much as I cared about her this relationship had turned into a no-win situation. It was evident through all this drama and was unable to see how I could help resolve her problems.
I thought soon all of this was going to be behind me. I would continue my classes at another University. Fawn hopefully would not be fired pending she got some help with her drinking. There would be no more fighting and arguing with her and no more sneaking around. It didn’t happen that easily. The plan we wanted went quickly South when Fawn told me she was pregnant. It was really no surprise. The unprotected sex never stopped. What were we made most of the decisions for us growing up. This wasn’t her fault for me thinking with my penis. We had numerous discussions about abortion, but we never came to an agreement and on campus things were getting more problematic.
Sometimes, when you can’t make a decision, the Universe will step in and make it for you, and it did. Fawn was on her usual drinking spree and was rushing to class. Coming down the stairs she missed a step and fell the rest of the way down. I was already gone so one of the neighbors saw her and ran to help. The ambulance was called and rushed her to the hospital. She suffered from a broken ankle, some cuts and scratches and most importantly the loss of the baby.
One of the staff from the office informed us that class was cancelled for the day due to Professor Denton being hospitalized. I had to be calm and cool and not bring attention to myself. Most of the class was looking in my direction. I waited until everyone had left and then Rick and I left for the hospital. When we entered the room, she was in tears and again it was a flashback. All I wanted to do was comfort and hug her. This is where all this shit began in the first place. Comforting her and hugging her. Couldn’t do it again!
Fawn did recover and the last time we spoke she was in Counseling for the drinking and the loss of the baby. She resigned from the University but did eventually teach again at another College. She taught me one thing from an Indigenous saying. “Beauty arises from living in harmony with the order of the universe”. Too bad we didn’t learn that together. Well, this is where one adventure or life lesson ended but, another a broken ankle, some cuts and scratches and most importantly the loss of the baby. I was bracing myself for the journey ahead. I had to grieve about the loss of my child and then regroup and move on. Somehow that is easier said than done.
As a closing note on this chapter of my life, it is important that I acknowledge the mental anguish of PTSD. Approximately, seven or eight out of every 100 people will have PTSD at some point in their lives. Women experience it more compared to men. Fortunately, half of the people with trauma can possibly recover within three months without treatment. I pray this be the case for Dr. Denton and me.

Remember at the beginning when I said mothers raise their daughters and spoil their sons? This is a reality. She didn’t do it on purpose. It’s just that they know how hard life will be for us Black boys in our society and that maternal instinct automatically kicks in and wants to love and protect them. These are some of the lessons that need to be taught and followed.

This Is a Letter to My Mom

Mama, stop coddling your sons. You taught your daughters everything on how to take care of themselves and be responsible. You almost made me dependent not just on you but, possibly other women that would want to take care me too. You told us we didn’t have to move out right away and we didn’t have to look for work right away. That’s why many of our men are still at home just like my brother.
When I misbehaved you made excuses for me. I was spoiled and felt entitled. Which was a mistake because I could never be entitled in this Country or any Country. You held me so close to you that it almost prevented me from going out into the world to experience life. Fortunately, I chose another path and left home.
Discipline for all children is a must. When dad tried to punish us, you always stepped in again and again. You wanted to shield us from getting our asses beat. We have to be accountable for our behaviors. Otherwise, we will not believe that we are or can be responsible for our negative actions.
Controlling our thought processes takes away our ability to think for ourselves and make life decisions. If we have not been given this opportunity, we will not be able to handle any crisis that may confront us. A Black man cannot afford to wait for someone else to think for us. We must be ready at any moment to handle our business with good judgement and make the right call.
Try not to be a helicopter mom by controlling our every move. We need to learn how to do things for ourselves if we’re going to survive in the real world. As sons, we understand you love us. When there is no structure, you hold us back from becoming men. We cannot become good husbands if we have not been taught.
Thank you, mama. You did what you thought was best. And I love you for that!

Twelve
